heading: MODULO PRIMO
card_summary: This chunk from the Eighth Tone of Cantico Terzo opens the "Primo Modulo" (First Module) of Zorzi's allegorical-cosmological commentary on the Heavenly Jerusalem described in Revelation 21. Working through John's vision of a city built from gold and precious stones, Zorzi interprets gold as the theological virtue of carità (charity), drawing on Paul's letters to the Romans and Corinthians, Psalm 50 (the Miserere), and the Book of Revelation (3:18). He then undertakes a lapidary reading of jasper (diaspro), drawing on Aaron, Pliny, Albertus Magnus, and the hermetic "secretiores philosophi," before proceeding to the twelve foundation stones, where he harmonises the Johannine vision with Moses' pectoral stones and Ezekiel's description of Tyre. The diamond's notable absence from sacred gemology is explicitly discussed. Sources cited include Pliny, Albertus Magnus, Hermes Trismegistus (Mercurius), and Paul.
essay:
## Overview

This summary covers the opening portion of the "Primo Modulo" section (pp. 2451–2484), specifically the first three numbered subsections visible in the opening 300 lines of the chunk. The text belongs to the *Tono Ottavo* (Eighth Tone) of the *Cantico Terzo* and proceeds as a sustained allegorical commentary on Revelation 21, the Heavenly Jerusalem.

## 1. Gold as Divine Charity (Concentus I, pp. 2451–2288)

Zorzi opens with John's description of the celestial city as built from gold and precious stones. The gold is not merely ornamental but structural: it serves as mortar, as foundation material, and as the city's transparent medium — so pure as to be *simile al cristallo limpido* ("like clear crystal"), following Rev. 21:18.

The section then delivers a natural-philosophical account of gold before pivoting to allegory. Zorzi draws on Pliny and Albertus Magnus for the physical properties of gold: its malleability (from one *denaro* d'oro one can now produce 124 laminae, versus 70 in antiquity), its resistance to all corrosives including sublimated arsenic, and its paradoxical relationship with fire — it liquefies but is not consumed, and emerges purer after repeated firing (*obrizum*). The etymology of *aurum* is briefly contested: either from Greek *ὁράω* ("to see," i.e., splendor) or from *aura* (brilliance), or from the name of its discoverer according to Hippocrates.

The allegorical pivot is direct and emphatic:

> *Aurum igitur vita est divina, sive spiritualis, vel charitas, quae vitam praestat, et qua decorantur, splendent, pulchrescunt, vivunt, et ditantur omnes hospites, et concentores illi.*

<cite>p. 2285 (Latin text, fol. LXXXVr–416v)</cite>

Gold is divine or spiritual life, that is, *caritas* — the charity that bestows life, adorns, illuminates, and enriches all the guests (hospites) and singers (concentores) of the heavenly dwelling. Zorzi immediately invokes Rev. 3:18 — the Archimusaeum (Christ) advising the purchase of *aurum ignitum* — as an exhortation to those hastening toward the celestial mansions.

The theological elaboration follows Paul systematically: Romans 8:35–39 ("Who will separate us from the love of Christ?"), 2 Cor. 11:20, and the Miserere (Ps. 50:14 — *redde mihi laetitiam salutaris tui*). The properties of gold are mapped point-by-point onto caritas:

- Gold is not consumed by fire → charity withstands tribulation and is purified by it
- Gold is not corroded by rust or acid → charity resists *rubigo peccati* ("the rust of sin") and *aerugo malitiae* ("the verdigris of malice")
- Gold resists all hammering → charity yields but is not broken (*cedit percutienti ... in huiusmodi coalescit*)
- Gold heals melancholy → charity gladdens the heart, heals spiritual ulcers

> *Statque invicta igni molestiae, ignique divino, sed in illo perficitur, in hoc autem augetur, et incrementum suscipit.*

<cite>p. 2286 (Latin text)</cite>

The solar symbolism appears explicitly: charity is "full of the force, beauty and splendor of the Sun beyond the world" (*vi quadam Phoebea*), conducting all things it fills to *perfectam consonantiam* — perfect harmony. The celestial garment of gold, Zorzi insists, is not a covering (there is nothing shameful to hide in the heavenly Jerusalem) but a transparent ornament.

## 2. Jasper (Diaspro) as the Walls — Concentus II (pp. 2288–2291)

Moving from gold to the structural material of the walls, Zorzi takes up jasper (*iaspis*). He draws on a wide lapidary tradition: Aaron (the Aaronic lapidary attributed to Aaron the High Priest), Pliny (*Naturalis Historia*), Albertus Magnus, and the *secretiores philosophi* ("more esoteric philosophers"). Seventeen varieties of jasper are noted (Aaron, Pliny, Albertus), of which Albertus names only ten.

The most important varieties are catalogued:
- Green and shining with red veins (the prime variety)
- Red translucent (like fire)
- Red opaque
- Smaragdine with a white line (*garamantica*)
- Multi-lined *polycalamos*, praised by the esoteric philosophers as beneficial to *concentores* (singers/harmonists)

Zorzi also cites Bocco, Tetel (Techel), Zenotemi (Zenothemis), and Arnoldo alongside Pliny and Albertus for the virtue of jasper: it stops blood, makes the wearer chaste and beloved, repels phantasms, and favors singers.

The allegorical reading is tight: jasper "enjoys both sexes" (*utroque sexu gaudet*), and this bisexual completeness figures the community of the redeemed — men and women alike — who praise the God who is, in Zorzi's citation of Hermes Trismegistus (*Mercurio*), *utriusque sexus foecunditate plenissimus* ("most full of the fecundity of both sexes"):

> *Decorque eius perbelle decorem omnium civium, et lapidum illorum ignitorum figurat, non enim coram summa pulchritudine aliquid nisi pulchrum, et decorum astare debet.*

<cite>p. 2289 (Latin text)</cite>

The variety of jasper types mirrors the variety of the blessed: Cherubim differ from Seraphim, Virtues from Powers, and — following Paul (1 Cor. 15:41) — one star differs from another in brightness. Prophets, patriarchs, apostles, martyrs, confessors, and virgins are different *genera* within the one *species* of the redeemed, just as Greeks, Italians, and Arabs belong to one human species. The power of jasper to repel phantasms figures the perpetual peace of the heavenly dwellers, freed from all troubling visions.

## 3. The Twelve Foundation Stones — Concentus III (pp. 2291–2293)

Zorzi turns to Rev. 21:19–20, listing the twelve foundation stones in order: jasper, sapphire, chalcedony, emerald, sardonyx, sardius, chrysolite, beryl, topaz, chrysoprase, jacinth, amethyst. Before proceeding to allegorize each stone he pauses to resolve two exegetical difficulties:

**First problem:** If "every precious stone" adorns the foundations (Rev. 21:19), why enumerate only twelve when many more precious stones exist?

**Second problem:** How can one building have twelve distinct foundations, each of a different stone, when foundations are normally of uniform material — and when Paul declares (1 Cor. 3:11) that no other foundation can be laid than Christ Jesus?

To the first, Zorzi responds that the twelve function as the *principalibus* (principal or representative) stones, the others being present but named by the dominant type. To the second, allegorically, the twelve foundations are the twelve Apostles, with Christ as the *firma petra* (firm rock), and the innumerable other *lapides vivi* (living stones) attached to them — as evidenced by Rev. 7:9: *"Et turba magna, quam dinumerare nemo poterat"* ("And a great multitude which no man could number").

A further distinction is introduced: not all stones prized by the world appear in sacred scripture. The diamond (*adamas*), cornelian (*achates*), *quiritia*, and others are absent from Moses' pectoral, Ezekiel's Tyre passage, and John's Revelation alike. This absence is significant: the diamond, most prized by worldly wealth, is *a viris divinis repudiatur* — "rejected by divine men."

> *Adamas enim, qui in tanto pretio est apud divites mundi huius, a viris divinis repudiatur.*

<cite>p. 2293 (Latin text)</cite>

This sets up the subsequent detailed allegorization of each of the twelve stones as symbolic of specific ranks, virtues, and harmonics within the heavenly choir — a project that presumably continues beyond the portion read here.

## Technical Terms and Sources

Key Latin/Greek terms introduced: *obrizum* (refined gold), *polycalamos* (multi-reeded jasper variety), *garamantica* (Garamantian jasper), *concentores* (singers/harmonists — Zorzi's signature term for the blessed), *lapides vivi* (living stones), *rubigo peccati* (rust of sin), *aerugo malitiae* (verdigris of malice).

Sources cited: Revelation (21:18–21; 3:18; 7:9), Paul (Romans 8; 1 Corinthians 3, 13, 15), Psalm 50, Hippocrates, Pliny (*Naturalis Historia*), Albertus Magnus, Aaron (lapidary), Bocco, Tetel/Techel, Zenothemis, Arnoldo de Villanova, Hermes Trismegistus/Mercurio, Ezekiel.

## Connection to Zorzi's Larger Project

The "Primo Modulo" continues Zorzi's sustained identification of *harmonia* with *caritas* — the bond that orders all things. Gold as the transparent mortar of the heavenly city recapitulates the cosmological argument of earlier sections: love/charity is the medium through which all created things achieve *perfecta consonantia*. The lapidary symbolism of the Apocalypse serves as a microcosm of Zorzi's larger encyclopedia of correspondences, linking natural philosophy (gemology, alchemy), scriptural exegesis, and Hermetic-Neoplatonic theology. The reference to Hermes Trismegistus on divine bisexuality, embedded within a commentary on Revelation, is characteristic of Zorzi's syncretistic Christian Kabbalah, integrating Hermetic prisca theologia within an orthodox Franciscan framework.
